$schemaNotes.phase.seconds: seconds for the phase; null ONLY when untimed is true
$schemaNotes.phase.untimed: true = the phase ends on user action (tap-to-stop) or the urge to breathe — never a timer. Pacer must not count down this phase.
$schemaNotes.phase.capture: 'retention' marks the untimed hold whose elapsed time is recorded per round as BreathworkRound.retentionSeconds
$schemaNotes.phase.group: phases sharing a group id repeat together as a unit; count lives in pattern.groups[id]
$schemaNotes.phase.label: display-only cue text
$schemaNotes.pattern.groups: map of group id -> { repeat, repeatMax?, label? }; repeat..repeatMax is a user-variable range
$schemaNotes.pattern.defaultCycles|defaultMinutes: exactly one is present: fixed-cycle patterns carry defaultCycles, run-for-duration patterns carry defaultMinutes
$schemaNotes.pattern.cycleLabel: display noun for one pass through phases; default 'cycle' ('round' for whm)
$schemaNotes.pattern.timingNote: honesty note where sources prescribe a shape or ratio rather than exact seconds
$schemaNotes.constitution: descriptions are descriptive only — what the pattern IS and where it comes from, never what it will do for you

patterns.name: Box breathing
patterns.description: Four equal 4-second sides — inhale, hold, exhale, hold on empty — an equal-count pacing pattern popularized by US Navy SEAL training.
cautions: If the empty-lungs hold feels straining, drop it and use triangle 4-4-4 instead — same pattern without the bottom hold.

patterns.name: 4-7-8 breathing

phases.label: Exhale fully through the mouth with a whoosh

patterns.timingNote: Weil: the 4:7:8 ratio matters, not the absolute seconds — speed the whole cycle up if the holds are hard, keep the ratio.
patterns.description: Andrew Weil's relaxing-breath pattern: a 4-count nasal inhale, 7-count hold, and 8-count audible mouth exhale, done in short sets of four cycles.
cautions: Light-headedness is common at first; Weil advises no more than four cycles per sitting for the first month of practice.
If holding is uncomfortable, shorten every phase but keep the 4:7:8 ratio.

patterns.name: Coherent breathing (5.5-5.5)
patterns.timingNote: 5.5s in / 5.5s out = 5.5 breaths per minute — the population-average resonance frequency, the breathing rate at which heart-rate oscillations synchronize with respiration. Individual resonance rates vary roughly 4.5-7 breaths/min; equal-length sides can be shortened or lengthened together.
patterns.description: Slow, even breathing at about 5.5 breaths per minute with equal inhale and exhale — the rate used in resonance-frequency (HRV biofeedback) protocols.
cautions: If 5.5 seconds per side feels long, shorten both sides equally rather than clipping the exhale.

patterns.name: Physiological sigh (cyclic sighing)

phases.label: Short second top-up inhale to fully inflate
Slow, full exhale through the mouth

patterns.timingNote: The Stanford study prescribed the shape (double inhale, then a slow full exhale longer than the combined inhales), not exact counts — the seconds here are approximate pacing.
patterns.description: A double nasal inhale followed by a long mouth exhale, repeated for about five minutes — the 'cyclic sighing' protocol from the 2023 Stanford study by Balban, Spiegel, and Huberman.

patterns.name: Extended exhale 4-6
patterns.description: Six breaths per minute with the exhale half again as long as the inhale — the exact 4-in/6-out timing studied as prolonged expiratory breathing.
cautions: Keep the long exhale unforced — straining to empty the lungs defeats the slow, easy pacing.

patterns.name: Triangle breathing
patterns.description: Box breathing minus the empty-lungs hold: inhale, hold, exhale at four counts each, flowing straight from exhale into the next inhale.

patterns.name: Wim Hof round

phases.label: Power breath in — deep, belly first, through nose or mouth
Let go — relaxed release, not forced fully out
Retention — after the final exhale, hold on empty lungs until the urge to breathe returns
Recovery breath — inhale fully to capacity

powerBreaths.label: 30-40 deep breaths at a steady personal pace

patterns.timingNote: Official guidance is 30 breaths per round and 3-4 rounds; power-breath seconds here are approximate pacing, and the retention hold is untimed and user-variable by design — its elapsed time is the captured metric (BreathworkRound.retentionSeconds).
patterns.description: The Wim Hof Method round: 30-40 deep power breaths, an untimed breath-hold on empty lungs (the retention), then a full recovery inhale held about 15 seconds — repeated for 3-4 rounds.
cautions: NEVER practice in or near water — breath-holding after heavy breathing can cause blackout without warning (shallow-water blackout).
NEVER practice while driving or standing, or anywhere fainting would be dangerous — always sit or lie down in a safe place.
Tingling, light-headedness, and dizziness are common; if they intensify or you feel faint, stop and breathe normally.
Loss of consciousness is possible in rare cases even when seated — do not push retention past a strong urge to breathe.
The official method advises against practicing during pregnancy or with epilepsy, and advises medical consultation first for cardiovascular or other serious health conditions.

patterns.name: Pursed-lip breathing

phases.label: In through the nose — 'smell the roses'
Out slowly through pursed lips — 'blow out the candles'

patterns.timingNote: American Lung Association guidance: exhale about twice as long as the inhale (2 in / 4-or-more out), through lips pursed as if whistling.
patterns.description: A 2-second nasal inhale followed by a 4-second exhale through pursed lips — the exhale-doubled pacing pattern taught in pulmonary rehabilitation.
cautions: Exhale gently through the pursed lips; never force the air out.
